1: 'And now, O Israel, listen to the statutes and rules I am teaching you to follow, so that you may live and go in and take possession of the land' — the wĕʿattāh ('and now') formula is the suzerainty-treaty transition from historical prologue (Deuteronomy 1-3) to stipulations (Deuteronomy 4-26). In Hittite treaties the historical prologue established the basis for obligation; the 'and now' pivot introduced the specific covenant demands. Moses's transition signals that the review of YHWH's acts was not merely historical reminiscence but the motivational foundation for the laws that follow. 'So that you may live' introduces the life-or-death consequence structure that pervades Deuteronomy's covenant theology.
2: 'You shall not add to the word that I command you, nor take from it' — the prohibition on addition and subtraction is the standard ANE canon-formula for authoritative legal texts. Hittite treaties, Mesopotamian law codes, and Egyptian royal inscriptions all contain similar prohibitions against alteration. The same formula appears in Hammurabi's epilogue and in Revelation 22:18-19 at the other end of the biblical canon. The formula functions to close the authoritative corpus: addition would authorize additional obligations; reduction would remove binding requirements. Both distort the suzerain's authentic demands.
3: 'Your eyes have seen what the LORD did at Baal-peor — the LORD your God destroyed from among you every man who followed the Baal of Peor' — the Baal-Peor incident (Numbers 25:1-9) involved Israelite men engaging in sexual immorality with Moabite women and worshiping a Moabite fertility deity associated with the Peor mountain. Twenty-four thousand Israelites died in the subsequent plague. The incident is cited here as the most recent case study in covenant infidelity with immediate fatal consequences — the audience's generation witnessed it. The appeal to living memory ('your eyes have seen') is the rhetorical foundation of the warning.
4: 'But you who held fast to the LORD your God are all alive today' — the survivors are identified by their covenant loyalty (haddĕbēqîm baYhwh ʾĕlōhêkem, those who clung to YHWH). The verb dābaqa (to cling, cleave) is the same used for marriage union in Genesis 2:24 and describes the covenant-loyalty bonding that the Shema demands (Deuteronomy 10:20; Deuteronomy 11:22; Deuteronomy 13:4). Survival at Baal-Peor was not random: those who remained in covenant relation with YHWH survived; those who defected died. The verse presents covenant loyalty as the literal difference between life and death.
5: 'I have taught you statutes and rules, as the LORD my God commanded me, so that you should do them in the land you are entering to possess' — the legislation of Deuteronomy is explicitly contextualized for the land: 'in the land you are entering to possess.' This land-Law link means that exile is not merely geographic displacement but the destruction of the social and legal conditions under which the covenant can be lived. The Mosaic statutes were designed for a specific covenant community in a specific territory — their full observance required the land, making the Exile the covenant's self-destructive consequence for land-violation (Deut 28).
6: 'Keep and carry out these statutes, for this will be your wisdom and understanding before the other peoples, who will hear all these statutes and say, "Surely this great nation is a wise and understanding people"' — the apologetic for Torah wisdom before the nations is remarkable: Israel's covenant law is presented as a public testimony to YHWH's character visible to the surrounding world. In the ANE context, wise law-giving was a mark of divine favor for a nation's king (Hammurabi's prologue claims Marduk's commission to 'bring forth justice in the land'). Israel's statutes are presented as surpassing the wisdom of any ANE law code because they originate with YHWH, not a human king.
7: 'What great nation has a god as close to it as the LORD our God is to us whenever we call upon him?' — YHWH's accessibility through prayer ('whenever we call upon him') is contrasted with ANE deities whose attention required elaborate cultic manipulation and whose responses were uncertain. In Mesopotamian religion, divine accessibility was mediated through complex omens, divination, and temple rituals whose outcome was never guaranteed. Israel's prayer theology — direct address, individual or communal, at any time — represented a structurally different relationship between deity and people: not appeasement of a distant power but communication with a near and responsive suzerain.
8: 'What great nation has statutes and rules as righteous as this entire law I am setting before you today?' — the comparative claim ('as righteous as') invites implicit comparison with the great ANE law corpora: Ur-Nammu (ca. 2100 BCE), Lipit-Ishtar (ca. 1900 BCE), Hammurabi (ca. 1750 BCE), Hittite laws, and Assyrian laws. These codes share surface similarities with Mosaic law (case-law format, lex talionis principles) but differ fundamentally in theological grounding: ANE codes are royal decrees serving the king's political legitimacy; the Mosaic Torah is YHWH's direct covenant with the entire people, making every Israelite — including the king — equally subject to the divine standard.
9: 'Be careful, and guard yourselves diligently, lest you forget the things your eyes have seen, and lest they pass from your heart all the days of your life. Teach them to your children and your children's children' — the memory-transmission obligation is one of Deuteronomy's primary pedagogical structures. The threat is not external attack but internal forgetting (šākaḥ). The antidote is deliberate cross-generational teaching. The Second Temple development of formal Torah education in synagogue contexts, the institution of bar mitzvah as covenant-entry, and the Passover Haggadah's structured retelling of the Exodus all represent institutionalized responses to this verse's teaching mandate.
10: 'Tell them about the day you stood before the LORD your God at Horeb' — the Horeb theophany (the Sinai event in Deuteronomy's terminology) is the foundational revelatory event that all subsequent generations must encounter through transmitted memory. The rabbinic tradition that 'all Israel stood at Sinai' — including future generations — derives from this kind of language: the Horeb experience was paradigmatic and representationally inclusive. Philo of Alexandria developed the Horeb theophany as a model of divine self-disclosure. The Christian parallel is the Eucharist and baptism as enacted memory of Christ's death and resurrection that makes past events present for each generation.
11: 'The mountain burned with fire up to the heart of heaven, wrapped in darkness, cloud, and deep gloom' — the Sinai theophany's triple atmospheric phenomena (darkness, ʿānān cloud, ʿărāpel deep darkness) combine natural storm imagery with the ANE conventions for divine appearance: gods appeared in storm, cloud, and fire throughout the Near Eastern world. The storm theophany was YHWH's primary mode of manifestation (Psalms 18:7-15; 29; Deuteronomy 50:3). The paradox of fire and darkness together is deliberate: YHWH is present and self-revealing, yet transcendent and unseen — the theological logic for the aniconic requirement that follows in Deuteronomy 4:15-18.
12: The Horeb prohibition of images is grounded in the nature of the Sinai theophany itself: 'you heard the sound of words but saw no form (temunah); there was only a voice' (Deuteronomy 4:12). Israel's aniconic tradition is historically distinctive — ancient Near Eastern religion was uniformly iconic: divine images (ṣalmu in Akkadian) were the physical locus of the deity's presence, 'activated' through mouth-washing and mouth-opening rituals. To worship without an image was anomalous. Greco-Roman observers noted it with derision: Tacitus writes that Jews 'acknowledge one God only ... of unbounded might, and eternal, neither admitting of any representative in human shape.' Deuteronomy grounds aniconism not merely in prohibition but in the nature of the Horeb revelation itself: YHWH is the God who speaks without being seen — the word, not the image, is the medium of divine self-communication.
13: 'He declared to you his covenant, which he commanded you to keep — the Ten Commandments — and he wrote them on two tablets of stone' — the Decalogue's designation as the bĕrît (covenant) rather than merely 'the law' is theologically significant: the Ten Commandments are the covenant document itself. The two stone tablets are the treaty-copy standard: ANE suzerainty treaties were always written in duplicate, one copy for each party. The tablets placed 'in the ark' (Deuteronomy 10:2, 5) follow treaty-archive custom: the treaty copy was stored at the god's temple. The tablets in the ark are Israel's treaty copy deposited before YHWH — a living legal document, not merely a religious display.
14: 'And the LORD commanded me at that time to teach you statutes and rules for you to carry out in the land you are crossing over to possess' — Moses's mediatorial role is explicit: YHWH spoke the Decalogue to the people (Deuteronomy 4:12-13), but commissioned Moses to teach the statutes and rules. This bifurcation — direct divine speech for the Decalogue, Mosaic mediation for the detailed legislation — reflects the narrative structure of Exodus 20-24 (Decalogue directly, Book of the Covenant through Moses). The Deuteronomistic tradition understands Moses as the covenant-interpreter par excellence, whose role the later prophets continued.
15: 'Therefore watch yourselves very carefully. Since you saw no form on the day the LORD spoke to you at Horeb out of the midst of the fire' — the conclusion drawn from Horeb's formless voice is the entire weight of the Second Commandment's reasoning. The logic is: since YHWH appeared without form, any image-making is a human invention imposed on divine self-disclosure, not a legitimate aid to worship. The Reformation tradition (Calvin, Institutes 1.11-12) built its anti-image theology directly on this verse's argument. The Deuteronomic logic also implies that idolatry is epistemologically dishonest: it misrepresents YHWH's own chosen mode of self-revelation.
16: 'Do not act corruptly by making for yourselves a carved image in the form of any figure — the likeness of male or female' — the prohibited image categories begin with anthropomorphic forms. The specific prohibition on human-form images contrasts with Egypt's ubiquitous anthropomorphic deity representations and the Canaanite male/female deity pairs. Asherah poles (female goddess symbol) and bull images (male deity power symbol) are the specific temptations Israel faced in Canaan. The verb 'act corruptly' (šāḥat) frames image-making not as innocent religious art but as covenant-violation that corrupts the worshiper's fundamental relationship with YHWH.
17: 'The likeness of any animal on the earth, the likeness of any winged bird that flies in the sky' — terrestrial and avian animal images are the second and third prohibited categories. Egypt's theology systematically depicted deities as animal hybrids: Ra as sun-disk or hawk, Anubis as jackal, Hathor as cow, Horus as falcon. The prohibition on animal images is a direct counter to Egyptian iconographic theology. The enumeration (human, animal, bird, fish) roughly follows the creation order of Genesis 1:20-27 — YHWH's creatures are not YHWH's representations; the creation cannot be an icon of the creator.
18: 'The likeness of anything that creeps on the ground, the likeness of any fish in the water beneath the earth' — the prohibition extends to creeping things and fish, completing the Genesis 1 creation categories. Serpent imagery was pervasive in Egyptian religious iconography (the uraeus cobra on pharaoh's crown, the Nehebkau serpent deity). Fish deities were prominent in the Philistine Dagon cult and Mesopotamian Oannes traditions. The comprehensive list forecloses any route of naturalistic image-making as legitimate worship: nothing in the created order may serve as YHWH's icon because YHWH disclosed himself as voice, not form.
19: 'Beware lest you lift your eyes to heaven and see the sun and moon and stars — the whole host of heaven — and be drawn away to bow down and serve them, things the LORD your God has allotted to all the peoples under the whole heaven' — the astral religion warning addresses the Mesopotamian star-worship that was the dominant religious technology of the ancient Near East. Babylonian astrology, the worship of Sin (moon), Shamash (sun), Ishtar (Venus), and the star-gods was the prestige religion of the imperial cultures. The remarkable statement 'the LORD has allotted to all the peoples under the whole heaven' (i.e., permitted the nations to worship astral bodies) distinguishes Israel's exclusive covenant with YHWH from the religious arrangements of other nations.
20: 'But the LORD has taken you and brought you out of the iron furnace, out of Egypt, to be a people belonging to his own inheritance, as you are today' — the 'iron furnace' (kûr habbarzel) is a metallurgical metaphor for Egypt's intensely purifying oppression: iron smelting required extreme heat and pressure. The same image appears in Jeremiah 11:4 and 1 Kings 8:51. Egypt was not merely slavery but the crucible in which Israel as a covenant people was formed. The metaphor also invokes YHWH's incomparability: no other god attempted to take one nation out of another by such means (cf. Deuteronomy 4:34). Israel's national identity was forged by the Exodus and belongs to YHWH as his inheritance.
21: 'Furthermore, the LORD was angry with me on your account and swore that I would not cross the Jordan' — the repetition of Moses's exclusion in the context of the image-prohibition warning is structurally deliberate: Moses's fate functions as the most vivid illustration of covenant seriousness. The exclusion of the covenant mediator himself demonstrates that covenant consequences apply without distinction of rank. YHWH's anger was triggered by the waters-of-Meribah failure (Numbers 20:10-12), which the Deuteronomic tradition frames as Moses's solidarity with Israel's rebellion — a representational consequence, not a personal one in isolation.
22: 'I must die in this land; I am not to cross the Jordan. But you shall cross over and possess that good land' — the contrast between Moses's death in Moab and Israel's crossing emphasizes the representational logic: Moses the leader dies bearing the representative consequence; the people receive the land. The brevity of the verse — three short clauses — mirrors the finality of the statement. The pattern anticipates Christ's death as the representative who does not enter the earthly possession but whose death enables his people's entry into the eschatological inheritance (Hebrews 3:5-6: Moses as servant vs. Christ as Son).
23: 'Be careful not to forget the covenant of the LORD your God that he made with you, and make a carved image in the form of anything the LORD your God has forbidden you' — the covenant-memory command positions image-making specifically as covenant forgetting: to make an image is to forget the Horeb theophany's formless voice-revelation. The covenant is not a set of abstract obligations but a specific historical relationship (YHWH who spoke from Horeb, who brought you from Egypt) — forgetting that history is the cognitive condition that makes idolatry seem reasonable and permissible.
24: 'For the LORD your God is a consuming fire, a jealous God' — the consuming fire metaphor (ʾēš ʾōkĕlāh) connects the Horeb fire-theophany (Deuteronomy 4:11-12) to YHWH's jealous exclusivity. In ANE contexts, divine jealousy (qannāʾ) is not a psychological flaw but the theological claim of exclusive relationship: YHWH alone is Israel's God, and Israel's exclusive loyalty is the covenant's foundational requirement. The same language appears in the Decalogue's Second Commandment context (Exodus 20:5). The Hebrews writer cites this verse (Hebrews 12:29) in the context of the new covenant's Zion-theophany, demonstrating the enduring force of the consuming-fire characterization.
25: 'When you have children and grandchildren and have grown old in the land, and if you act corruptly by making a carved image' — the conditional clause ('when you have grown old in the land') anticipates the pattern of prosperous complacency leading to covenant violation — a theme the Deuteronomistic History traces through every Israelite king after Solomon. Prosperity and length of tenure in the land breed the memory-failure that produces idolatry. The warning is addressed specifically to the generation-after-generation future, not merely the immediate audience, because the danger grows with time rather than diminishing.
26: 'I call heaven and earth as witnesses against you today that you will quickly perish from the land' — calling heaven and earth as covenant witnesses is an ANE treaty convention attested in Hittite suzerainty treaties (which invoked gods, mountains, rivers, heaven, and earth as witnesses). Since no other deity above YHWH could serve as covenant witness for Israel, heaven and earth function as impersonal cosmic witnesses. The same invocation appears in Deuteronomy 30:19; Deuteronomy 31:28; Deuteronomy 32:1 (Moses's song calls heaven and earth to hear). The witness invocation makes covenant violation a publicly attested breach before the whole cosmic order.
27: 'The LORD will scatter you among the peoples, and you will be left few in number among the nations where the LORD will drive you' — the exile prophecy of Deuteronomy 4:27-28 is one of the earliest and most explicit in the Deuteronomic tradition. The scattering language (hēpîṣ, to scatter/disperse) is the technical term for diaspora throughout the Deuteronomistic History and the prophets (Jeremiah 9:16; Ezekiel 12:15; Deuteronomy 20:23). The prophecy's fulfillment in the Assyrian deportation of the northern kingdom (722 BCE) and the Babylonian deportations (605, 597, 586 BCE) gave Deuteronomy 4's warnings retrospective force as covenant documentation of what the exile meant.
28: 'And there you will serve gods made by human hands — wood and stone — that can neither see, nor hear, nor eat, nor smell' — the idol polemic ('cannot see, hear, eat, smell') is the standard Israelite critique of images, developed extensively in Isaiah 40:18-20; Deuteronomy 44:9-20; Psalms 115:4-8; Deuteronomy 135:15-18. The irony is pointed: Israel will be forced to live in proximity to the impotent idols their own covenant-breaking pushed them toward. The four verbs (see, hear, eat, smell) invert four sensory activities YHWH performs in relation to Israel: he sees their affliction, hears their cry, provides food, accepts their offerings. Dead idols are the anti-YHWH.
29: 'But from there you will seek the LORD your God and you will find him, if you search for him with all your heart and with all your soul' — the restoration promise in the midst of the exile warning is one of Deuteronomy's theological pillars. The two key verbs — bāqaš (to seek) and māṣāʾ (to find) — describe diligent pursuit that results in genuine encounter. The condition 'with all your heart and with all your soul' employs the Shema's vocabulary (Deuteronomy 6:5) applied to the exile-seeker: the total-person devotion demanded in covenant loyalty is also the mode of repentance that leads to restoration.
30: 'When you are in distress and all these things have come upon you, in the latter days you will return to the LORD your God and listen to his voice' — the phrase 'in the latter days' (bĕʾaḥărît hayyāmîm) is eschatological language marking a future decisive moment. In context it refers to the post-exilic restoration, but the phrase carries forward-pointing force throughout the Prophets (Isaiah 2:2; Jeremiah 23:20; Ezekiel 38:16; Daniel 10:14). Paul reads Deuteronomy's exile-and-return cycle as the prototype for the eschatological gathering of all Israel (Romans 11:25-27). The cycle of rebellion-exile-return is the structural spine of the Deuteronomistic History.
31: 'For the LORD your God is a merciful God. He will not abandon you or destroy you or forget the covenant with your fathers that he swore to them' — raḥûm (merciful, compassionate) is the tender maternal-care term derived from reḥem (womb). The three divine commitments — not abandon, not destroy, not forget the covenant — are the positive counterpart to Israel's failures. The patriarchal covenant (sworn to the fathers) is the theological bedrock beneath Israel's repeated failures: the Abrahamic covenant is the unconditional grant upon which the conditioned Mosaic covenant rests. When Israel's Mosaic obligation fails, YHWH's Abrahamic commitment endures.
32: 'For ask now about the former days that were before you, since the day God created mankind on the earth, and search from one end of heaven to the other: has any such great thing as this ever happened?' — Moses challenges his audience to test YHWH's incomparability by universal historical survey. The rhetorical scope ('from the day God created mankind ... from one end of heaven to the other') frames the following questions about the Exodus and Sinai revelation as uniquely unprecedented in all of human history — not merely impressive in Israel's experience but without parallel in universal human experience. This is the most comprehensive apologetic claim in Deuteronomy.
33: 'Did any people ever hear the voice of God speaking out of the midst of fire, as you have heard, and survive?' — the claim that Israel survived the Horeb fire-theophany reflects both the ANE convention of divine fire as mortal danger and the specific Horeb miracle. Mesopotamian and Egyptian texts associate divine fire-theophanies with death-consuming holiness. Israel's survival of direct divine address from fire was understood as a unique miracle — YHWH's accommodation of his transcendent holiness to the capacity of human hearing. The Psalmist and the Prophets reflect on Horeb's fire as the archetype of covenant encounter (Ps 29; Hab 3).
34: 'Or has any god ever attempted to go and take one nation out of the midst of another nation — by tests, by signs and wonders, by war, by a mighty hand and an outstretched arm, and by great terrors — like all that the LORD your God did for you in Egypt before your eyes?' — the five-fold description of the Exodus mechanisms (tests, signs and wonders, war, mighty hand, outstretched arm, great terrors) is one of the most comprehensive catalogs of divine saving action in the OT and becomes the formula for reciting the Exodus throughout Deuteronomy and the Psalms. The six-element formula in varying combinations appears in Deuteronomy 6:21-22; Deuteronomy 7:8; Deuteronomy 9:26; Deuteronomy 26:8; Psalms 136:12; Jeremiah 32:21 — it became the liturgical template for remembering YHWH's saving work.
35: 'To you it was shown so that you would know that the LORD is God; there is no other besides him (ʾên ʿôd)' — ʾên ʿôd ('there is no other') is the most absolute monotheistic formula in the OT and becomes the signature affirmation of Second Isaiah (Isaiah 45:5, 6, 14, 18, 21, 22; Deuteronomy 46:9). The phrase functions here as the conclusion of the historical-theological argument: the Exodus and Horeb events were pedagogical demonstrations designed to teach Israel the uniqueness of YHWH. The knowledge is not philosophical deduction but revelatory demonstration: 'to you it was shown so that you would know' — divine display serves an epistemological purpose in covenant education.
36: 'Out of heaven he let you hear his voice to discipline you, and on earth he showed you his great fire, and you heard his words out of the midst of the fire' — the spatial distinction between heavenly voice and earthly fire describes YHWH's transcendence-in-presence: the voice originates in heaven (YHWH is not localized to the mountain) while the fire phenomenon is earthly and observable. The verb yissar (to discipline, instruct) reveals the purpose of theophany: not terror for its own sake but formation of the covenant people's knowledge and obedience. The heavenly origin of the divine voice distinguishes YHWH from chthonic or mountain deities of the ANE.
37: 'And because he loved your fathers and chose their descendants after them, he brought you out of Egypt himself, with his great power' — the theology of election is grounded in prior covenantal love (ʾāhab) for the patriarchs. The word 'chose' (bāḥar) is the technical Deuteronomic election term: YHWH's selection of Israel is not based on Israel's merit (Deuteronomy 7:7-8: 'it was not because you were more in number ... it is because YHWH loves you and keeps the oath that he swore to your fathers'). The phrase 'himself, with his great power' emphasizes YHWH's personal agency in the Exodus — not through an intermediary but directly.
38: 'To drive out before you nations greater and mightier than you, to bring you in and give you their land as an inheritance, as it is today' — the dispossession of larger nations is the practical evidence of YHWH's committed power on Israel's behalf. The phrase 'as it is today' is the Deuteronomic historian's formula for events already completed from the writing perspective — the same formula used at Deuteronomy 2:22; Deuteronomy 3:14; Deuteronomy 4:20, 38. It signals that the text is written after the conquest, using historical fulfillment as a pedagogical tool: what Moses predicted, YHWH delivered, validating Moses's warnings and promises for the future.
39: 'Know therefore today, and take it to heart, that the LORD is God in heaven above and on the earth beneath — there is no other' — the repeated ʾên ʿôd formula (first at Deuteronomy 4:35, now at Deuteronomy 4:39) functions as the chapter's theological inclusio and its climactic affirmation. The addition of 'in heaven above and on the earth beneath' expands the claim from uniqueness to cosmic sovereignty: no domain exists — neither the celestial realm where ANE astral deities operated nor the earthly realm where territorial gods held sway — in which YHWH is not the sole God. This verse is the theological summit of Deuteronomy 4's extended apologetic argument.
40: 'Therefore keep his statutes and commandments that I am giving you today, so that it may go well with you and with your children after you, and so that you may live long in the land that the LORD your God is giving you for all time' — the concluding exhortation connects the theological argument (YHWH is uniquely God) to its practical consequence (covenant obedience). The incentive formula ('so that it may go well ... and so that you may live long') is Deuteronomy's standard motivation structure: covenant obedience produces flourishing; covenant violation produces destruction. 'For all time' (kol-hayyāmîm) frames the land-tenure as YHWH's perpetual gift, contingent only on sustained covenant loyalty.
41: Moses sets apart three cities of refuge east of the Jordan 'so that a manslayer could flee there.' The cities-of-refuge institution is developed in Numbers 35:6-34 and Deuteronomy 19:1-13. The three Transjordanian cities are established here even before the western Jordan cities are possible — Moses can only designate what lies within Israel's current holdings. The institution reflects the ANE principle of asylum: temples and designated sites provided refuge from blood-avengers. The Mosaic adaptation channels the asylum principle into a legal institution requiring adjudication of intent (premeditated vs. accidental homicide) before the manslayer's status is determined.
42: 'Anyone who kills his neighbor unintentionally without prior enmity — and by fleeing to one of these cities could live' — the distinguishing criterion for the cities of refuge is intent: 'without prior enmity' (bĕlî-daʿat, without knowledge/intent) identifies the unpremeditated killing. The avenger-of-blood system (gōʾēl haddām) was the pre-institutional mechanism for addressing homicide in tribal societies; the cities of refuge created a legal buffer between killing and automatic retribution, allowing for judicial determination. The protection of the unintentional killer reflects the Mosaic legal principle that punishment must be proportionate to intent.
43: 'Bezer in the wilderness on the tableland for the Reubenites; Ramoth in Gilead for the Gadites; and Golan in Bashan for the Manassites' — the three Transjordanian cities of refuge correspond to the three tribal territories established in Deuteronomy 3:12-17: Bezer for Reuben's southern territory (modern site uncertain, probably in the Madeba plateau), Ramoth-Gilead for Gad's central hill country (modern Tell Ramith in northern Jordan), and Golan for Manasseh's northern Bashan territory (modern Sahm el-Jolan). These cities are matched by three western cities in Deut 19 (Joshua 20:7: Kedesh, Shechem, Hebron), completing a network of six asylum cities across the entire territory.
44: 'This is the law that Moses set before the people of Israel' — the colophon sentence (wĕzōʾt hattôrāh ʾăšer-śām mōšeh lipnê bĕnê yiśrāʾēl) functions as a formal heading or subscription for what follows. The term tôrāh here refers not to the entire Pentateuch but to the specific covenant instruction that begins in chapter 5 with the Decalogue. The colophon signals a structural transition: chapters 1-3 were historical retrospective; chapter 4 was theological argument for covenant loyalty; chapters 5-26 are the detailed covenant stipulations Moses transmitted.
45: 'These are the testimonies, the statutes, and the rules that Moses spoke to the Israelites after they came out of Egypt' — the three-fold legal vocabulary (ʿēdōt, testimonies/ordinances; ḥuqqîm, statutes; mišpāṭîm, rules/judgments) is Deuteronomy's standard legal taxonomy. Testimonies (ʿēdōt) are covenant-stipulations bearing witness to YHWH's character; statutes (ḥuqqîm) are fixed ordinances, often cultic; rules (mišpāṭîm) are case-law judgments derived from precedent. The subscript 'after they came out of Egypt' situates the entire Deuteronomic law as Exodus-consequence: the redeemed people receive the redeemer's requirements.
46: 'East of the Jordan in the valley opposite Beth-peor, in the land of Sihon king of the Amorites, who lived at Heshbon' — the geographic specificity — Beth-peor, Sihon's land, Heshbon — situates the law within the narrative of conquest: Moses speaks from territory already won. The mention of Beth-peor echoes 3:29 and anticipates Deuteronomy 4:3's Baal-peor warning; the juxtaposition of the covenant-renewal location with the site of Baal-Peor apostasy is consistent Deuteronomic irony. 'The land of Sihon' frames the present location as evidence of YHWH's faithfulness in the immediate past, grounding the authority of the law being given.
47: 'They had taken possession of his land and the land of Og king of Bashan — the two Amorite kings east of the Jordan' — the summary identification of the two Transjordanian victories (Sihon and Og) as the territorial basis for Moses's teaching location recapitulates chapters 2-3. The phrase 'the two Amorite kings east of the Jordan' becomes a standard Deuteronomistic shorthand for the first-fruits of the conquest (Joshua 2:10; Deuteronomy 9:10; 1 Kings 4:19). The concluding geographic summary frames the entire body of Moses's addresses as given from the firstfruits of YHWH's promised land.
48: 'From Aroer on the rim of the Valley of the Arnon as far as Mount Sirion — that is, Hermon' — the south-to-north geographic bracket (Aroer on the Arnon in the south, Hermon in the north) reprises the territorial summary of Deuteronomy 3:8. Mount Sirion as the Sidonian name for Hermon was already noted in Deuteronomy 3:9. The repetition of the geographic bracket in the colophon (Deuteronomy 4:44-49) creates a formal envelope: the law's setting is precisely defined. The Aroer-to-Hermon range describes the full extent of Transjordanian territory over which Moses spoke — won from Sihon and Og, distributed to Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh.
49: 'Along with all the Arabah east of the Jordan as far as the Sea of the Arabah, under the slopes of Pisgah' — the colophon's final geographic marker, the Arabah and Pisgah, closes the retrospective precisely where chapter 3 ended: Moses's Pisgah vision (Deuteronomy 3:27) and the encampment at Beth-peor (Deuteronomy 3:29). The geographic frame of the entire Transjordanian section (Deuteronomy 1-4) is thus Pisgah: the mountain from which Moses viewed what he could not enter. The concluding mention of the Sea of the Arabah and Pisgah completes the boundary description of Reuben, Gad, and Manasseh's territory and marks the formal close of the historical-theological prologue before the Decalogue begins in chapter 5.
